topic: launch expecting a result vs fire-and-forget
snippet:
Scenario A (fire-and-forget):
MainActivity launches SettingsActivity.
MainActivity does not expect anything back — it just waits for SettingsActivity to eventually finish.

Scenario B (expecting a result):
MainActivity launches PhotoPickerActivity SPECIFICALLY to receive a chosen photo back.
When PhotoPickerActivity finishes, it hands data back to MainActivity.
question: What's the key relational difference between Scenario A and Scenario B?
choices: There is no real difference — every launched activity always sends data back to its caller automatically

In Scenario B, MainActivity and PhotoPickerActivity have a two-way relationship: MainActivity is waiting to receive something specific back, whereas in Scenario A the relationship is one-directional — launch and move on
Scenario B is impossible; only the OS can pass data between activities, never one activity directly to another
Scenario A can only happen with activities from the same app, while Scenario B only works across different apps

explanation.summary: Not every activity launch is the same kind of relationship. Sometimes the caller only cares that the other screen was shown (fire-and-forget). Other times, the caller specifically depends on getting something back — a chosen photo, a confirmed setting, a scanned code — turning it into a genuine two-way relationship rather than a one-way handoff.
lineByLine: Scenario A: MainActivity → launch → SettingsActivity. MainActivity moves on without expecting anything specific in return.
Scenario B: MainActivity → launch (expecting a result) → PhotoPickerActivity. MainActivity is effectively paused, waiting.
When PhotoPickerActivity finishes, it hands a result back specifically to MainActivity — completing the two-way relationship MainActivity initiated.
explanation.tip: Recognizing which kind of relationship a launch represents helps you read app behavior correctly: does the calling screen appear to 'wait' for something (a result relationship), or does it move on freely (fire-and-forget)? Common examples of the result relationship: picking a photo, scanning a barcode, or confirming a permission choice.

snippet:
MainActivity shows a confirmation Dialog: "Delete this forecast?"

Back stack before Dialog appears: [ MainActivity ]
Back stack while Dialog is showing:  [ MainActivity ]  <- unchanged, still just one entry
question: A confirmation dialog appears on screen, visually on top of everything else. What's its relationship to the Activity back stack discussed earlier in this chapter?
choices: The Dialog is a separate Activity, so it gets pushed onto the back stack just like ForecastDetailActivity did earlier
The Dialog is not a separate Activity at all — it's an overlay drawn on top of MainActivity's existing content, so the back stack is unaffected and still shows just one entry
The Dialog replaces MainActivity in the back stack until it's dismissed
Every visible UI element, including dialogs, must correspond to its own manifest-declared Activity

explanation.summary: Not everything that visually appears on screen represents a new entry in the Activity back stack. A Dialog is drawn on top of the current Activity's existing window — it doesn't require its own manifest declaration and doesn't add a new stack entry.
lineByLine: Before the Dialog: `[MainActivity]` — one stack entry.
Dialog appears: still `[MainActivity]` — the same single entry, just with an overlay drawn above its content.
Dismissing the Dialog doesn't 'pop' anything from the back stack, because nothing was pushed onto it in the first place.
explanation.tip: This distinction matters for reading app behavior correctly: if pressing 'back' simply dismisses something without revealing a different screen underneath, it was likely an overlay (Dialog, BottomSheet, Snackbar) rather than a separate Activity. True back-stack navigation always reveals a different full screen when popped.
